# Transcrição: Lesson 02 - The 1 Percent Rule - Why Small Habits Make a Big Difference

- **Fonte:** Lesson 02 - The 1 Percent Rule - Why Small Habits Make a Big Difference
- **Duração:** 03:02
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

As we start the course, I'd like to start by getting us on the same mindset and philosophy about what kind of habits we're trying to build and why those are important. And in order to do that, I'd like to tell you the story of Dave Brailsford and the British cycling team. For about a hundred years, the British cycling team had been incredibly mediocre. They had only won a single gold medal way back in 1908. They hadn't performed very well on the world stage and cycling's premier race, the Tour de France, they had never won a single Tour de France. And around the mid-2000s, when they were looking to buy an entirely new set of bikes for the British cycling team, a huge order of 100 or 200 bikes, they contacted the best bike manufacturers in the world and they weren't even allowed to get a quote because the manufacturers didn't want other teams to know that the British cyclists were using their bikes. So they were incredibly average from the standpoint of the world stage. And when they brought this guy named Dave Brailsford in around 2003, 2004, they had this challenge that they laid out to him. They said, Mr. Brailsford, we'd like to change this, right? We want to be competitive for once. We want to be competitive on the world stage. What's your plan for doing so? And Brailsford believed in this concept that he called the aggregation of marginal gains. And the way that he described it was the 1% improvement in nearly everything that you do. So they started with a bunch of things that you would expect a cycling team to focus on. They got slightly lighter tires on their bike. They had a more ergonomic seat. They had their outdoor riders wear indoor racing suits, which are lighter and aerodynamic. But then they made a bunch of changes that you wouldn't expect a cycling team to make. They did things like split tests, different types of massage gels to see which one led to the best muscle recovery. They taught their riders how to wash their hands to reduce the risk of infection. They even figured out the best type of pillow for each rider to get a good night's sleep and then brought that on the road with them to hotels. And Brailsford said, if we can actually execute on the strategy, if we can make all these little 1% changes related to cycling, then I think that we can win a Tour de France in about five years. He ended up being wrong. They won the Tour de France in three years and then they repeated again with another rider in the fourth year. But it was at the Olympics in 2008 after doing this for about five years that Brailsford's strategy really started to come to fruition. At the 2008 Olympics in Beijing, the British cyclists won 70% of the gold medals available. At the 2012 Olympics in London, they won 60% of the gold medals available. And at the 2016 Olympics in Rio, they again won 60% of the gold medals available. And this idea that 1% improvements, that small gains, can make not just a little bit of difference, not just a cherry on top of our habits and behavior, but actually a very substantial difference, that it can be the difference between succeeding at the highest level possible and having average performance. That's something that I want us to take with us throughout the rest of this course. This is a core philosophy that small habits and tiny gains, that little changes add up to something really significant. And I'd like you to keep that in mind as we think about the habits that we want to build. What we're looking at is trying to figure out ways to make these small habits reinforce and accumulate upon one another and build into something really significant. So keep that in mind and I'll

## [03:00 - 03:02]

see you in the next lesson.
